reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware inference from a single reported comparison (time-to-thermal-runaway under matched overcharge rate and environment) and separates admissible timeline conclusions from tempting but unsupported mechanistic/energetic extrapolations. Decoys are plausible and mostly single-fault, though C’s protection-threshold claim may invite minor implementation assumptions and E risks sounding responsibly cautious, slightly reducing discriminative sharpness among some experts.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware reasoning about abuse-mode specificity (CID overcharge protection vs thermal abuse) and SOC-driven regime shifts in NCA hazard severity, with correct options staying within the stated observations. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though two share a similar overgeneralization pattern and some wording (e.g., “indicates governing mechanism changes character”) could feel slightly interpretive, reducing neutrality and discrimination at the margin.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware inference from chemistry- and abuse-mode-dependent peak temperatures to defensible safety/design conclusions, while penalizing mechanistic and system-level overclaims. Decoys are plausible and largely single-fault, targeting distinct extrapolation errors (mechanism attribution, system safety generalization, and proxy misuse). Discrimination is strong for experts, though some correct options rely on moderately interpretive language about “exothermic intensity” and “system sizing,” slightly weakening boundary tightness.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware reasoning about what SOC management can and cannot guarantee across chemistries and abuse modes, forcing an expert to separate severity modulation from hazard elimination. It also elicits recognition that “non-ignition” is not equivalent to “no hazard” and that overcharge pathways require distinct protections, which provides strong discrimination against superficial mitigation claims.
